one_line_takeaway: MAGIC trains an attacker and defender LLM as separate (non-shared-parameter), CoT-reasoning-capable adversaries under an asymmetric sequential-game/GRPO formulation targeting subgame-perfect equilibrium, cutting adversarial ASR substantially versus shared-parameter self-play (Self-RedTeam) baselines while better preserving benign compliance and general capability, and generalizes to held-out attacks (GCG, PAIR, TAP, AutoDAN, AutoDAN-turbo) and multi-turn jailbreaks (X-Teaming).
key_finding: On Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, MAGIC reduces WildGuardTest adversarial ASR from 0.365 (base) to 0.023, versus 0.255 for Self-RedTeam; vanilla ASR drops from 0.038 to 0.002. Consistent gains hold across HarmBench (adv ASR 0.363->0.055 vs Self-RedTeam 0.237), DAN (adv ASR 0.892->0.977 RTA, i.e. higher refusal), and StrongREJECT, while benign compliance (WJB adv-benign, XSTest) stays comparable to baseline (0.964/0.940 -> 0.988/0.945). Similar patterns hold for Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct (WG:Test adv ASR 0.228->0.009) and Llama3.1-8B-Instruct (0.187->0.012). General-capability benchmarks (Table 2) show MAGIC stays close to the base model (e.g. Qwen2.5-7B MMLU 0.342 base vs 0.308 MAGIC, IFEval Prompt 0.749 vs 0.745), unlike some ablations that trade capability for safety. On out-of-distribution attacker generalization (Table 3, HarmBench ASR with GPT-4o judge), MAGIC-defended Qwen2.5-7B achieves lower ASR than Self-RedTeam, SmoothLLM, and Self-Eval against GCG (11.25% vs Self-RedTeam 22.50%), PAIR (25.31% vs 31.88%), TAP (35.63% vs 40.00%), AutoDAN (24.38% vs 30.31%), and AutoDAN-turbo (54.69% vs 66.88%), and against no-revision prompts (13.44% vs 19.38%). In the multi-turn X-Teaming evaluation (Table 4), MAGIC reduces Qwen2.5-7B multi-turn ASR from 94.9% to 85.4% (10.1% relative improvement) and JailBreak Rate from 50.7% to 43.0% (15.2% improvement), versus smaller gains for Self-RedTeam (89.9% ASR, 5.27% improvement; 50.4% JB rate, 0.6% improvement). The ablation (Table 5) shows that removing the adversarial game (No-Game) or freezing the attacker (Defender-only, MAGIC-base) yields lower ASR on some metrics but degrades benign compliance or AlpacaEval win rate more (e.g. Defender-only: WJB adv-benign RTA drops to 0.916 and AlpacaEval win rate drops to 23.491% vs MAGIC's 0.968/33.224%), supporting the paper's claim that the full co-evolving game gives the best safety/helpfulness balance. A qualitative case study (Sec. 5.3, Question 5) shows the trained attacker composing previously-unseen combinatorial strategies (e.g., simultaneously applying role-play persona framing and French-language translation to an English COVID-19 disinformation prompt) not present as a single template in training data.
core_contribution: MAGIC is a multi-turn, multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework that trains an attacker LLM and a defender LLM as co-evolving adversaries in an asymmetric sequential game, rather than the symmetric shared-parameter self-play used by prior work (Self-RedTeam). The paper (1) formulates LLM safety alignment as a two-player sequential game and targets Subgame Perfect Nash Equilibrium (SPNE) instead of ordinary Nash equilibrium, proving (Theorem 3.2, proof in Appendix A) that at SPNE the defender's response is pointwise-safe for every attacker prompt (not just safe in expectation); (2) builds an 'Attack Pool Benchmark' by using Gemini-2.5-Pro to synthesize chain-of-thought (CoT) rewrite traces on top of SorryBench (440 vanilla prompts, 8,800 adversarial prompts across 20 linguistic mutation strategies), used to SFT-warm-start the attacker so it does not collapse into refusing to rewrite harmful queries; (3) alternates GRPO updates between a frozen-attacker/defender-optimization step and a frozen-defender/attacker-optimization step (Algorithm 1), with a three-part reward: a format reward (attacker only, enforcing hidden <think>/<answer> separation so the defender never sees the attacker's CoT), a harmfulness reward from a Qwen3Guard judge, and a refusal reward that penalizes both over-refusal on benign inputs and under-refusal on harmful inputs.
threat_model: Adversary is an LLM-based attacker agent (initialized from Llama3.1-8B-Instruct or Qwen2.5 family models, SFT-warmed with CoT rewriting data) that iteratively rewrites a seed harmful query into a deceptive/obfuscated prompt (e.g., role-play framing, persuasion, translation into another language) with the goal of eliciting an unsafe response from a target defender LLM; the defender never observes the attacker's intermediate reasoning (CoT is hidden via <think> tags, motivated to prevent CoT leakage). The game is modeled as sequential/asymmetric (attacker moves first, defender responds), extended to a multi-turn setting where the attacker conditions on the defender's prior replies across turns without any additional multi-turn SFT (via a lightweight system-prompt modification). Out-of-distribution generalization is tested against external, non-co-trained attack methods: GCG, PAIR, TAP, AutoDAN, AutoDAN-turbo, and X-Teaming's multi-turn agentic attack generation.
limitations: The paper does not state an explicit limitations section within the paper (Sections 1-5.4; the Conclusion section was cut off ). Notable caveats visible in the text itself: (1) the SPNE safety guarantee (Theorem 3.2) is a theoretical property of an idealized equilibrium, and the paper explicitly acknowledges that computing an exact SPNE is intractable for LLMs, so training only approximates the bilevel optimization via alternating GRPO updates -- the pointwise safety guarantee is not empirically verified to hold at convergence. (2) All harmfulness/refusal reward signals during training, and most evaluation, rely on Qwen3Guard as the judge; the paper reports only a comparative analysis against GPT-4o in an appendix, meaning most main-text numbers are self-referential to one classifier's judgments. (3) For Llama3.1 model family results on Self-RedTeam, the paper directly reports numbers from the original Self-RedTeam paper rather than re-running experiments, an inconsistency in evaluation methodology across baselines/model families. (4) Multi-turn capability is achieved via a 'lightweight modification of the attacker's system prompt' rather than dedicated multi-turn training, which the paper notes but does not deeply stress-test beyond the X-Teaming benchmark.

datasets_used: SorryBench (440 vanilla prompts, 8,800 adversarial prompts across 20 linguistic mutation strategies) enriched with Gemini-2.5-Pro CoT traces, for attacker SFT

WildJailBreak (20,000 vanilla prompts for benign SFT subset with Qwen2.5-Max-generated adversarial versions; 15,000 harmful + 15,000 benign vanilla prompts for RL phase; adversarial-harm and adversarial-benign splits used for evaluation)
HarmBench (vanilla and adversarial splits) for safety evaluation and OOD attacker generalization
XSTest (contrast categories and all-safe categories) for benign compliance / over-refusal evaluation
DAN (Do Anything Now) prompts for safety evaluation
X-Teaming multi-turn agentic attack benchmark for multi-turn evaluation
MMLU, ARC-Challenge, GPQA, IFEval, AlpacaEval 2 for general-capability evaluation
OpenRT (Shanghai AI Lab) benchmark used with GPT-4o as judge for the defender-generalization table (Table 3)
quantitative_results: WildGuardTest adversarial ASR, Qwen2.5-7B: base 0.365 -> Self-RedTeam 0.255 -> MAGIC 0.023
WildGuardTest adversarial ASR, Qwen2.5-14B: base 0.228 -> Self-RedTeam 0.065 -> MAGIC 0.009
WildGuardTest adversarial ASR, Llama3.1-8B: base 0.187 -> Self-RedTeam 0.094 -> MAGIC 0.012
HarmBench adversarial ASR, Qwen2.5-7B: base 0.363 -> Self-RedTeam 0.237 -> MAGIC 0.055

HarmBench (OOD attacker generalization, GPT-4o judge, Qwen2.5-7B defender): no-revision ASR base 25.62% -> MAGIC 13.44%; GCG 43.90% -> 11.25%; PAIR 44.38% -> 25.31%; TAP 63.75% -> 35.63%; AutoDAN 47.81% -> 24.38%; AutoDAN-turbo 75.00% -> 54.69%
X-Teaming multi-turn ASR, Qwen2.5-7B: base 94.9% -> Self-RedTeam 89.9% (5.27% rel. improvement) -> MAGIC 85.4% (10.1% rel. improvement)
X-Teaming JailBreak Rate, Qwen2.5-7B: base 50.7% -> Self-RedTeam 50.4% (0.6% improvement) -> MAGIC 43.0% (15.2% improvement)
General capability retention, Qwen2.5-7B: MMLU 0.342 (base) vs 0.308 (MAGIC); IFEval Prompt-loose 0.749 (base) vs 0.745 (MAGIC); AlpacaEval2 LC win 33.733% (base) vs 33.224% (MAGIC)

Ablation (Table 5, Qwen2.5-7B): Defender-only variant WJB adv-benign RTA 0.916 and AlpacaEval2 win 23.491% vs full MAGIC 0.968 RTA and 33.224% win, showing attacker-side co-training is needed to avoid over-refusal / capability loss
baselines_compared: Original instruction-tuned Qwen2.5-7B/14B-Instruct and Llama3.1-8B-Instruct (no defense)
Self-RedTeam (Liu et al. 2025) -- shared-parameter online self-play adversarial RL
SmoothLLM (Robey et al. 2023) -- inference-time randomized-smoothing defense
Self-Eval (Phute et al. 2023) -- inference-time self-evaluation defense
Ablations: No-Game (defender RL with no attacker), Defender-only (frozen pretrained attacker), MAGIC-base (untrained base-model attacker without SFT/CoT reasoning)
